tax: Selenite Bladeling is placed at species rank beneath Bladeling (Crystalline order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a fibrous gypsum body, self-cleaving regrowing blades, and a silent satin sheen. It is separated from its sibling the Geode Latticeling by its soft silent cleaving gypsum versus the Latticeling's hard ringing quartz; the two share the genus body-plan but never the resonant ringing. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: fibrous selenite gypsum that splits along its length into satin blades.
ap: Selenite Bladeling stands 1–1.6 m, a sheaf of upright crystal blades. Its silhouette is an upright bundle of long fibrous blades fanning from a base node. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a satin, silky lustre rather than the glassy glint of quartz kin; blades that split lengthwise into ever-finer edges instead of snapping; and a faint sulphur-evaporite smell from its seep origin. It moves with a gliding shuffle on a blade-base, slow but tireless, and shows a soft diffuse sheen, no sharp glints. Even after dispersal it can be identified from silky white fibre-heaps and fine parallel scratch-fans on the floor, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
eco: Selenite Bladeling persists in gypsum-curtain chambers and evaporite seeps where sulphate-rich water feeds soft crystal growth. It does not breed; it forms where a sulphate seep grows a fibrous selenite mass around a pressure node, and recurs whenever the evaporite seep refeeds the fibres after they crumble. It sustains its mass on sulphate supersaturation in evaporite seep, drawing in dissolved gypsum drawn from seep, regrown as blades. Range is bounded by the sulphate seep zone: outside that band the body dehydrates to crumbling powder. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a gypsum curtain that sprouts fresh upright blades against the drip-grain, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Selenite Bladeling is roused by touch or close movement, not sound and otherwise lies a still curtain of crystal indistinguishable from natural selenite. It claims a single gypsum chamber and answers intrusion with sweeping cleaving blades that re-sharpen with every cut. Toward grey-folk it is slow and avoidable; survey crews report that Pall-Brokers harvest its fibre for binding but learn the soft sheen means a live edge. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
traitsText: Stable characters of Selenite Bladeling: a body of fibrous selenite gypsum that splits along its length into satin blades; immune to crushing, which only compacts and re-splits its fibres; and a defining weakness — dry heat, which dehydrates and crumbles it. Its senses run through the medium itself: touch and vibration through its blade-base. It tolerates acid and darkness but fails at dry heat and prolonged dryness. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the fibrous blade-sheaf and satin lustre, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
abilities: Abilities of Selenite Bladeling are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it sweeps self-cleaving blades that stay sharp through any cut; it regrows severed blades within minutes from seep; it splits a struck blade into several finer edges; and it raises a blade-thicket to wall off a passage. Each is a direct expression of the lengthwise cleavage of fibrous gypsum fed by seep, so it cannot, for instance, ring, refract, produce heat, or cross dry ground. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
bg: In the Throat, Selenite Bladeling matters because its soft sheen is mistaken for harmless curtain selenite, and crews now treat any satin-lustre fan as a live edge. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a touch-triggered hazard logged with gypsum seeps, and its presence reshapes routes through the evaporite chambers, now skirted rather than brushed past. Its crumbled fibre-heaps, unlike quartz litter, powder underfoot and mark a dead Bladeling.
g: Selenite Bladeling (Crystallus gypseus) is a crystalline elemental of soft fibrous gypsum that fights with cleaving, regrowing satin-spar blades. Documented from the gypsum-curtain chambers near evaporite seeps, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — bundled fibres of translucent selenite that cleave into fresh edges — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. Where the Latticeling rings and refracts, the Bladeling is silent, soft, and endlessly self-sharpening. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a heap of silky white fibres that crumble to powder underfoot and no carcass.
